You can hold, exchange, and spend in 40+ currencies at the real interbank exchange rate—the same rate banks use between themselves—often 2–3% cheaper than traditional banks or exchange offices. Spend abroad or withdraw cash, and you pay almost zero fees; it’s like having a local bank account wherever you go. Sending money overseas is fast, transparent, and costs a fraction of what you’d pay elsewhere, with transfers often arriving in seconds.

I am beyond impressed with Revolut as my primary banking partner. It is flexible, fast, and reliable 24/7. Compared to traditional banks that often limit you to a single currency per account, Revolut offers massive value by allowing you to hold and exchange multiple currencies directly within the app.

Why it beats traditional banks:
Cost-Efficient: The combination of free transfers within the EU and no monthly maintenance fees (on the Standard plan) is a game-changer for my finances.

Superior Security: The security features are miles ahead of local banks. Being able to instantly freeze cards and enable location-based security for ATM withdrawals gives me total peace of mind.

Customer-Centric: While my relatives still have to waste time visiting physical bank branches to fix errors—and get charged for the "privilege"—I handle everything from my phone in seconds. Revolut respects my time.

Beyond Simple Banking:
Revolut truly excels by putting everything in one place:

Investing: Seamless access to stocks and crypto assets within the same interface.

Rewards: The RevPoints program adds extra value to every transaction.

Savings: The recently introduced Flexible Accounts (Savings) with competitive interest rates and near-instant access to funds show that they are truly a user-oriented bank.

Verdict: If you are tired of the slow, expensive, and outdated systems of traditional local banks, Revolut is the future. It’s smart, secure, and incredibly efficient.

During all this time, I only had two unpleasant situations.

The first one happened at an ATM abroad. The machine didn’t provide the full amount of cash I requested. To be fair, I believe it was more the ATM’s fault than Revolut’s. I contacted their support team, and after the investigation, the issue was resolved and I received my money back within around 60 days (if I remember correctly).

The second situation was linked to the renewal of my bank card. Due to some technical issue during the delivery process, I unexpectedly received 3 or 4 identical Revolut cards at my home address under my name. It was clearly a system or dispatch error, but again, nothing dramatic in the end.

Apart from these two situations in nearly a decade of use, everything has been faultless. The account has worked perfectly for me in different countries and for everyday payments, travel, online shopping, transfers, and cash withdrawals. I genuinely cannot complain.

Now, in May 2026, Revolut has evolved even more, with a banking licence and many additional features inside the app (most of which I personally don’t even use yet). Still, it’s reassuring to know that the innovation is there whenever needed.

The platform is well designed and clearly appreciated by users with high activity levels, especially for international operations.

However, this review specifically refers to REVOLUT BUSINESS, not the personal account.

Initially, the service was very attractive due to low fees and the absence of fixed monthly costs. Even without intensive use, it showed strong potential.

With the changes introduced in 2026, a minimum monthly fee of around €10 has been applied even to low-usage or inactive accounts. This fundamentally changes the service model, turning it from a flexible, low-cost solution into one with fixed costs that are not always justified.

While this may work well for businesses with high transaction volumes, it is not suitable for users who need the account occasionally or as a secondary solution. In such cases, the fixed monthly fee significantly reduces overall value compared to traditional accounts with usage-based pricing.

Another critical point is balance handling: the account can go negative due to automatic subscription fees, even without available funds. This may be unexpected for users who assume a “balance-only” system where operations are simply blocked when funds are insufficient.

In summary: potentially a strong solution for high-volume users, but no longer competitive for low or occasional usage after the introduction of fixed fees.

As a loyal Revolut user who transitioned from Gold to Ultra on day one, I expected a "next-level" lifestyle integration. Instead, my experience with Revolut Mobile has been a masterclass in technical dysfunction and customer service negligence.

The Critical Failures
The 48-Hour Blackout: Upon signing up, my eSIM failed to activate for two full days. In a world where your phone is your lifeline for work and safety, being unreachable for 48 hours is an unacceptable "teething issue."

The Content Block Loop: I am currently trapped behind a content block. When I try to resolve it, the system redirects me to Vodafone.

The "Not Our Customer" Paradox: This is where the frustration peaks. Revolut tells me to contact Vodafone to fix the block; Vodafone (rightfully) tells me I am not their customer because my contract is with Revolut. I am stuck in a digital "no man’s land" with no one taking ownership.

Support: AI Hallucinations & Lack of Human Care
Despite being an Ultra customer —which promises priority support—the service has been abysmal:

Dishonest AI: The Revolut support bot has repeatedly claimed that "tickets have been raised" and "specialists are looking into it." It’s clear now these are automated placations; days pass with zero updates and zero resolutions.

Passing the Buck: Human agents seem untrained on the mobile product, consistently trying to offload technical accountability to Vodafone, despite Revolut being the service provider.

The PAC Code Irony: It is telling that the only efficient process Revolut Mobile has mastered is letting you leave. After days of "waiting for a specialist," my PAC (Porting Authorisation Code) was issued in under three minutes.

The Verdict
Revolut is a fantastic bank, but they are a failing mobile virtual network operator (MVNO). They have launched a product without the infrastructure to support it, leaving their highest-paying customers stranded.

Warning to Ultra Members: Do not rely on this for your primary SIM. If things go wrong, you will be caught in a circular loop of blame between Revolut and Vodafone, while the AI lies to you about progress.

Pros: Fast PAC code delivery.
Cons: Everything else.

Status: Leaving for a provider that actually knows I exist.

Due to this experience, I no longer feel confident using Revolut for situations where errors can occur, as resolving them requires too much time and effort.

My company switched to Revolut Business earlier this year after having used Monzo Business exclusively since the company’s creation back in 2023. Based on strong word of mouth and recommendations from other business associates, my company basically went ‘all in’ on Revolut Business even using them as our merchant account for credit card processing. We were initially very pleased with Revolut Business.

This all changed two days ago when we were suddenly notified that our merchant account was being closed immediately and unilaterally, without any possibility of appealing the decision. This came without warning and the news was delivered in a brusque email that announced the account’s closure matter of factly. This in turn triggered other account problems because we were then notified that our business account, always previously held in good standing, was now being randomly targeted for a ‘security review’. Our account was automatically signed out of Revolut’s app and we were locked out with no possibility of us having any access to the accounts during the review that has now gone on for two days. My company has various agreements and commitments with other vendors and customers and whilst this review is ongoing all direct debits have been stopped as well as any scheduled payments that were made. It has taken a great deal of energy trying to ensure that important payments like automobile insurance for our company cars do not get cancelled for non payment amongst other annoying payment foibles caused by this review, This is wreaking havoc on our commitments and is tarnishing our good name, and quite frankly, I am flabbergasted that my own bank is the one causing us so much trouble.

Revolut Business advertises their offerings as being a primary banking solution for businesses, but I now question whether it was wise to entrust all our account needs to an app-based fintech bank rather than a traditional ‘bricks and mortar’ bank. At the very least with the latter, I could complain in person about such an onerous ‘security review’, but with Revolut I cannot. Caveat Emptor.

In March 2026 I made a payment through Revolut to my tradesman who was standing beside me and verified the phone number on screen before I pressed send. Revolut’s system sent €500 to a complete stranger (another Revolut Customer)in the United Kingdom instead.

I spent 16 hours in chat with Revolut across multiple agents and managers on the day of the incident and the days that followed. During that time, two separate managers independently acknowledged that “the system should have matched the exact number” and that “it seems there was a mismatch.” Despite these admissions, Revolut refused to use the reversal powers explicitly available to them under Section 21 of their own Irish Branch Personal Terms, which state they can reverse transactions credited following an error on their side.

One agent told me reversing the funds was “impossible without breaking the law.” In the same chat, the same agent confirmed Revolut was “actively working with the beneficiary to reverse the funds.” These two statements were made in the same conversation by the same agent.

When I asked an agent to clarify the terms that applied to my situation, I was directed to a version of Revolut’s terms that were not yet in existence at the time of the incident - future terms provided in place of the terms that were actually applicable on the date the payment was made. This is the standard of information

I received across 16 hours of interaction with a regulated financial institution.
I submitted a formal complaint on 3 March 2026. It was not acknowledged within the required timeframe despite three separate emails. I was passed between multiple agents on multiple occasions and asked to repeat the same information every time.

I am on certified medical leave for work-related stress with a reduced salary. The €500 was payment to a tradesman for urgent essential repairs to my home.

Rather than honouring their own contractual obligations, Revolut’s response to a vulnerable customer was to tell me to pursue a complete stranger in a different country through the Gardaí - while simultaneously withholding the recipient’s telephone number under GDPR.
They shared his name and his full UK address but refused to provide his phone number - the single piece of evidence that would have confirmed whether their system had mapped the same number to two different accounts, exactly as their own managers admitted.

In other words, Revolut directed me to investigate the matter myself in another jurisdiction while deliberately withholding the information I would need to do so.

Revolut’s own terms state clearly that if money is not received by the intended recipient they will refund the payment. Their own managers admitted a system mismatch. Their own agents contradicted each other repeatedly across 16 hours of chat. Their agents provided incorrect terms. And their response to all of this was to leave a vulnerable customer on medical leave to pursue a stranger in another country alone.

The recipient still has my €500. Revolut are aware of this. They have the power under their own terms to act and have chosen not to. Instead they are knowingly protecting a dishonest recipient while directing a vulnerable customer to investigate the matter herself in a different jurisdiction without the information needed to do so.

I have filed complaints with the Financial Services and Pensions Ombudsman and reported this matter to the Central Bank of Ireland. I am sharing this experience across all social media platforms so that no other vulnerable customer is subjected to what I have experienced at the hands of a regulated financial institution that had both the power and the contractual obligation to protect me and chose not to.

I’m honestly shocked at how poor Revolut’s review system has become.

I tried to create a custom card design using a simple philosophical quote, no logos, no branding, no copyrighted images and it was rejected for “trademarked/copyrighted content.” That’s objectively incorrect.

What’s worse is that there is clearly no meaningful human review involved. The entire process feels like an automated system flagging things blindly, with customer support unable (or unwilling) to actually review or override the decision. I was given vague, copy-paste responses that didn’t address the actual issue at all.

If Revolut is going to rely this heavily on automated moderation, there needs to be a real escalation path when the system gets it wrong. Right now, there isn’t one.

It’s frustrating to see a company at this level hide behind generic policies instead of offering real support or accountability. This experience made it very clear that when something goes wrong, you’re not dealing with people, you’re dealing with a system that doesn’t think.

Revolut seriously needs to fix this.

This is not just poor service, it’s a fundamental misunderstanding or disregard of a regulated switching process.

As a result, I’ve been exposed to missed payments, potential charges, and unnecessary stress. Under the guarantee and the Direct Debit Guarantee, Revolut are fully liable for any financial loss, yet instead of taking ownership, they’ve deflected responsibility.

I have now escalated this as a formal complaint and will be taking it to the Financial Ombudsman Service if it is not resolved immediately.

If you’re considering switching to Revolut, be very clear: when it works, it may be convenient. When it fails, you are left doing their job for them while carrying all the risk.

This is not a minor issue. This is a breakdown of a regulated banking process, and it should concern anyone trusting them with their finances.

Extremely negative experience with Revolut. My case revealed what appears to be a systemic issue: the bank’s claimed “customer protection” does not match reality. At the initial stage, they create the impression that they will assist and properly investigate the issue, but once all evidence is provided, the process effectively stops — cases are closed without proper review, leaving the customer alone with the problem.
In my case, I provided a complete set of evidence: proof of defective goods, proof of return shipment, confirmed delivery back to the merchant, and full communication history. Despite this, the bank refused to initiate a chargeback, relying on formal and unsubstantiated reasons. Critically, they ignored the actual nature of the dispute — a “Returned Goods – Refund Not Processed” case — which is a clearly defined category under card scheme rules.
It is particularly concerning that the bank:
misclassifies disputes or ignores their actual nature
refuses to apply the appropriate card scheme rules
fails to provide a specific reason code or legal justification
disregards submitted evidence without explanation
acts without proper due diligence
Such conduct raises serious concerns regarding violations of consumer rights and failure to fulfill obligations as a card issuer. In practice, the bank is refusing to perform its core function — protecting the cardholder in disputed transactions.
Additionally, customer support communication is generic and superficial. Responses lack substance, contain no references to Visa rules or internal procedures, and appear to be template-based rather than based on actual case analysis. This strongly suggests that decisions are made without a proper review of the facts.
It gives the clear impression that in complex or high-value disputes, the bank simply chooses not to act in order to avoid responsibility. As a result, the customer is left without both their funds and any meaningful protection.
I strongly warn other users: do not rely on this bank in situations where you need real protection of your money. If an issue arises, you risk being ignored, dismissed, and left without any effective support.

I am a Premium user and Revolut and its support hasn't stopped disappointing me ever since I became their client . This time it's their handling of CFD trading that has shocked me, even though I thought that nothing with Revolut will be able to do it more. These systematic failures have already caused me direct financial losses and left me exposed to further losses.

Across multiple incidents, I was unable to close open leveraged CFD positions. Close orders were rejected by the system, and in some cases the “Close position” function was unavailable, even while the instrument appeared tradable and prices were updating.

At the same time, risk-management tools failed:
– stop-loss could not be placed or did not trigger as expected
– repeated attempts to reduce exposure were declined
– positions remained open against my intention

This resulted in forced exposure to market risk, including holding leveraged positions into the weekend when I explicitly attempted to exit.

Revolut support acknowledged that orders were being declined by the system and linked the issue to technical problems. However, the complaints department later denied any technical issue and relied on generic explanations such as volatility, slippage, and “protective mechanisms”.

These explanations do not address the core issue.

Refusing or failing to execute exit orders is not slippage. It is a breakdown of execution.

In my view, this raises serious concerns regarding:
– the reliability of order execution
– the ability of clients to perform basic risk management
– transparency of execution conditions and order handling

These are fundamental expectations under EU investment service standards, including principles commonly associated with MiFID II such as best execution, fair treatment of clients, and the ability to manage risk on leveraged instruments.

I also observed:
– inconsistencies between displayed prices and execution outcomes
– lack of clear explanations for rejected orders
– contradictory responses between support and the complaints department

Despite documented issues, the complaint was rejected and no meaningful remedy was offered beyond a small goodwill refund unrelated to the actual trading impact.

This is not about a single trade. It is a repeated pattern of execution problems and inconsistent explanations.

If you are considering using Revolutas a bank, broker or G-d forbid for CFD trading, you should be aware that in critical moments the platform may and will fail to execute orders, while responsibility for resulting losses remains entirely on the client. 0 trust, 0 reliability, 0 customer support.

I am beyond frustrated with Revolut's complete lack of buyer protection. They market themselves as a modern banking alternative, but when things go wrong with a merchant, they leave you completely stranded.
Most recently, I paid €33 for an Express Shipping service that was guaranteed to arrive at my home in 2 working days with a signature required. Instead, the service was a total failure: it took 6 days to arrive and was dumped at a local shop without any attempt to deliver to my house or get my signature.
Even though I have proof that the service I paid for was not provided, Revolut has been difficult and dismissive about the dispute.
This isn't the first time, either. A couple of years ago, I bought an online course costed me €250 where the seller failed to deliver what was promised. I never received the course materials, yet when I raised a dispute and provided the details, Revolut refused to help me. After they declined the claim, they simply ignored my follow-up messages.
It seems Revolut is happy to take your money, but the moment you need them to step in and investigate a clear breach of contract or a failed delivery, they hide behind automated bots and excuses. If you value your money and want a bank that actually protects you from bad sellers, stay away from Revolut.

outgoing transfers blocked
merchant payments disabled

No timeline.

This has caused direct financial damage to our business and disrupted our operations entirely.

I made a purchase through a RevPoints offer (Intrepid Travel) clearly displayed as active in the Revolut app, with no visible geographic or operational restrictions.

The bonus points were not credited.

When contacting support, I received multiple contradictory explanations:

* first, that the campaign was not active in Luxembourg
* then, that the issue was due to not using the in-app browser
* finally, that the offer was active, but I did not meet conditions that were never clearly disclosed before the purchase

This raises serious concerns around transparency and potentially misleading commercial practices.

The case was handled by Muhammad, who acknowledged the confusion caused by previous responses but still refused to credit the points.

A compensation (1 month of Metal subscription) was actually granted.

As a customer, I relied on an offer clearly visible in the app when making a purchase. If key conditions are not properly disclosed or if tracking mechanisms fail, the responsibility should not fall entirely on the user.

I would expect Revolut to review this case more carefully and ensure that promotions are clear, consistent, and reliably applied, in line with basic consumer protection standards.

I had one of the most frustrating account opening experiences I’ve ever had with Revolut.

I attempted to open a business account for my LLC, which is used exclusively to receive 1099 income as a private contractor. I met all verification requirements except for the “Nature of Business” documentation.

I clearly explained, both in my application and multiple times to support, that I do not issue invoices because my work is tracked through the company’s internal portal, and I am paid based on hours logged through their system via Gusto. My 1099/pay documentation includes both my business name and the paying company’s information, and payments are sent directly to my business account.

Despite explaining this repeatedly, support kept asking for documentation that simply does not exist for my business model:

* A website/domain for my LLC (there is none, as this LLC exists only to receive contractor income)
* Invoices from my LLC to the company paying me (again, not how this arrangement works)

They even asked for my LinkedIn profile, and after initially saying that would be acceptable, later reversed course and said it was not enough.

I spent approximately 1.5 hours going back and forth with support, repeatedly explaining the same situation, only to be told I needed documents I had already made clear I could not provide. I even explained that I have a non-compete and cannot publicly advertise the business itself.

I chose Revolut because their features looked great and their pricing was competitive, but the onboarding experience was so rigid, inconsistent, and frustrating that I ultimately gave up.

I would not recommend them based on this experience.

Very disappointing experience with Revolut Business.

I opened a chargeback for several Amazon transactions related to an account that was unexpectedly closed, making it impossible to use the purchased goods (including digital content).

The dispute was closed internally without ever being submitted to the card network (Visa/Mastercard), effectively preventing any independent review.

Throughout the process, I received inconsistent and contradictory communication:
• I was initially told that I would receive documentation via email
• Later, I was informed that such documentation does not exist
• No reason code or detailed technical explanation was ever provided

In the end, the decision was justified with a very generic statement (“does not meet the criteria”), without any verifiable details.

For a Revolut Business account, I expected a much higher level of transparency and professionalism, especially considering the impact on my business.

Overall, the experience has been marked by poor communication, lack of clarity, and insufficient support.

I am now seriously considering moving my business elsewhere.

EDIT: I would like to clarify that I was never contacted by Revolut as stated in their reply. The only communication I received was their formal complaint response, which did not provide any concrete or technical explanation regarding the decision.

Given the lack of transparency, the contradictory information provided during the process, and the fact that the chargeback was never submitted to the card network, I have already escalated the matter to the Arbitro Bancario Finanziario (ABF), including paying the required fee myself.

I remain open to a proper resolution, but at this stage I expect clear and consistent communication rather than generic responses.

EDIT2: Following their public reply, Revolut contacted me via email. However, the outcome was exactly the same: no new information, no technical explanation, and no real attempt to resolve the issue.

The response simply repeated that the decision was “final”, without addressing any of the concerns previously raised or providing any concrete justification.

This confirms my initial experience: communication is inconsistent and ultimately inconclusive, especially when dealing with more complex cases.

As a result, the matter has now been escalated externally.

Revolut are again asking for more details via Trustpilot, even though everything has already been provided multiple times.

At this point, it feels less like progress is being made and more like effort is going into responding to reviews rather than resolving the actual issue.

There is still no clear update or resolution, and the situation remains ongoing with no real progress.

High CFD fees can lead to unexpected losses for users

I want to share an important warning for anyone thinking about trying CFD trading.

At first, everything looks simple and easy to use. You might think you’re just opening a small trade to test things. But what many users don’t realize is how the fees actually work.

The platform charges around 0.25% when you open a trade and another 0.25% when you close it. That might sound small, but it’s not calculated on your deposit — it’s calculated on the full position size, including leverage.

This means if you open a large position, the fees can become very high very quickly. Even if the market barely moves, you can lose a noticeable amount of money just from fees alone.

There are also additional costs if you keep trades open overnight.

This kind of trading is not suitable for beginners or anyone who does not fully understand how leverage and fees work together.

Please take the time to fully understand the cost structure before placing any trades. It can prevent unexpected losses.

I am publicly documenting my case because Revolut has refused to comply with basic PSD2 obligations and repeatedly avoided providing mandatory evidence regarding an unauthorised Apple Pay transaction.

1. What happened
My Apple ID and device were compromised by an unauthorised third party.
The attacker manipulated my Apple account and used Apple Pay to perform a transaction that I did not authorise, initiate, or authenticate.

I provided Revolut with clear evidence of:

Apple account takeover,

device manipulation,

unauthorised access,

suspicious activity preceding the transaction.

2. Revolut’s handling of the case
Despite receiving new, material evidence, Revolut:

provided no Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) evidence,

provided no biometric authentication logs,

provided no proof that I authenticated the transaction,

did not assess the documented Apple account takeover,

did not explain why a suspicious‑flagged transaction was processed,

repeatedly responded with emotional statements (“I feel bad”, “I hope you understand”) instead of regulatory answers,

incorrectly claimed that N26 was the merchant.

Screenshots attached show Revolut agents stating:

“the outcome remains final”,

“there is nothing else we can add”,

“certain aspects are beyond our control”.

None of these statements address authentication or PSD2 requirements.

3. Confirmation from N26
N26 has formally confirmed:

they are not the merchant,

they are not involved in the transaction,

they cannot escalate or refund it,

they have no role in authorisation.

This directly contradicts Revolut’s explanation and shows that Revolut misclassified the transaction.

4. PSD2 concerns
Revolut appears to be in breach of:

Article 72 PSD2 – obligation to prove authentication,

Article 73 PSD2 – liability for unauthorised transactions,

Article 74 PSD2 – obligation to provide SCA evidence,

the duty to reassess a case when new evidence is presented.

5. Escalation
Due to Revolut’s refusal to provide SCA evidence or reassess the case, I have escalated the matter to:

Banco de España,

Lietuvos Bankas,

FIN‑NET,

and relevant consumer protection bodies.

All screenshots of Revolut’s responses are included as evidence.

I want to share my experience to raise awareness about a scam I experienced while using Revolut.

I lost £5,835.37 through a task-based job scam where I was contacted via messaging apps and offered commission for completing online tasks. Initially, small withdrawals were allowed, which made the opportunity seem legitimate. Later, I was asked to make larger payments to unlock tasks and clear a “negative balance,” and I continued under pressure from the scammer.

Revolut did flag some transactions and showed warnings, and in some cases delayed payments. However, I was still able to proceed with multiple high-risk payments during a very short period of time.

I reported the case to Revolut, Action Fraud, and the Financial Ombudsman Service, but unfortunately the funds could not be recovered and my complaint was not upheld.

While I understand that I authorised the payments, I feel that stronger intervention or safeguards could help prevent similar situations, especially when customers are under pressure from sophisticated scams.

I am sharing this to warn others:
Please be extremely cautious of any job or online opportunity that asks you to send money first, especially if it involves “tasks” or mentions a “negative balance.”

I hope my experience helps others avoid going through the same situation.

I was initially very excited to use Revolut because the app’s design and interface look great. Unfortunately, I haven’t actually been able to use any of its features because I have been stuck in a verification "drama" for over a month that has left me feeling lied to and discriminated against.

The core of the issue is that I am non-binary, and my Mexican ID (INE) and CURP reflect this with an "X" gender marker instead of "M" or "F". From the very first day (February 6th), I told support exactly why the automated system was flagging my ID. Instead of addressing this, I was gaslighted for weeks. Support agents repeatedly insisted my high-quality photos were "blurry" or "not clear". They even gave me nonsensical advice like "checking if the app has camera permissions"—as if I could have sent them photos of my ID without the camera working. At one point, an agent even claimed the entire verification system was down, which would mean no one in the country could open an account.

I have dealt with many government agencies and institutions as a non-binary person, and even the most inefficient government offices have never treated me with this level of dismissiveness. While other fintech companies like Wallbit and ARQ (formerly Dolarapp) had the exact same initial issue with my CURP, their support teams solved it manually in less than a week.

In contrast, Revolut opened a ticket for me on February 25th. After weeks of checking in and being told they "couldn't provide a timeline," the ticket was finally closed yesterday with a response that was essentially a slap in the face: they confirmed they can't verify me and said it might be fixed in a "future update". No date, no solution, just an indefinite wait for a basic manual verification that other apps handled in days.

To make matters worse, when I finally gave up and tried to close my unverified account, the app gave me an error message. Support then told me they could close it if I sent a "selfie with a note"—meaning they are perfectly capable of verifying my identity manually to close an account, but they refuse to do the same to let me use mine.

I am writing this to warn others: do not trust this company with your money. Even if you manage to get verified, the support is incredibly inefficient and will resort to gaslighting rather than actually solving a technical problem. If you value your time and expect to be treated with basic dignity, look elsewhere.

After a nerve-racking week dealing with Revolut support, my account was closed based on claims that I submitted “false information,” which is completely unfounded.

The situation started when a support agent incorrectly claimed that my bank statement did not contain my full address. This was simply not true, yet that claim triggered the entire process that followed.

Based on this incorrect assertion, my account was closed on the grounds that I had “not submitted all required documents.” I was then told to submit an appeal.

The appeal form itself had an extremely short time limit, forcing me to rush just to attach the minimum files before it timed out. I immediately reported this issue through chat, and agents allowed me to submit additional documents there. I was assured that everything would be reviewed as part of the appeal.

For clarity, the documents I submitted included:
-Electricity bills showing my name and full address (for the past two months)
-Last month's bank statement with name and full address
-Temporary protection residence permit, issued in 2023 in Portugal and valid until March 2027 under EU and Portuguese law
-Latest tax declaration from the Finanças portal, including proof of submission

These are all official documents issued by legitimate Portuguese institutions. They contain identifying information, serial numbers, and are easily verifiable.

Despite this, I kept receiving push notifications asking me to resubmit my residence permit — a file that the system would not actually accept. Every time I contacted support, the agent would begin by telling me my account was closed, ignoring my appeal and supporting documents. After “checking,” they would then say they did not currently need any documents from me, despite the pending notification requesting them.

I repeatedly explained that this situation would obviously lead to my account being closed again if the issue was not properly relayed to the appeals team. Each time, different agents assured me they understood and that nothing further was required from me.

Eventually, a new agent entered the chat and simply insisted that my account had already been closed despite the ongoing appeal, claiming everything had been duly reviewed. This was surprising given that, in previous conversations, agents often seemed unfamiliar with the situation and unclear about what had already been submitted.

When I insisted on being given a legitimate reason for such a decision while the appeal was still supposedly under review, I was finally told that Revolut “has grounds to believe that some of the information submitted was not true.”

This is extremely concerning. First they incorrectly claim my bank statement does not show my full address, then they close my account for “missing documents,” then they request an appeal and additional proof, and after I provide multiple official, verifiable documents they claim the information might be false.

I have kept full transcripts of my conversations with support and records of all the documents submitted. Given the seriousness of accusing a customer of submitting false information, I would expect a proper review of this case and a clear explanation of what evidence led to this conclusion.

As a legal resident under temporary protection, I find the handling of this situation particularly troubling and incomprehensible.
I will be submitting the full documentation and chat transcripts to the relevant financial ombudsman for investigation.

P.S. Revolut’s reply here is a generic statement that does not address the core issue raised in my review.
My account was originally closed after a support agent claimed that my bank statement did not contain my full address. This claim was simply false, yet it became the basis for stating that I had “not submitted all required documents.” The subsequent appeal process did not address this error and instead concluded with the statement that Revolut “has grounds to believe some of the information submitted was not true,” without explaining what information was allegedly false.
They have failed to operate faithfully within the local context: my residence permit (for Ukrainians) remains legally valid under EU and national law until March 2027. In Portugal these permits are not reissued with updated dates, and institutions rely on the active legislation confirming their validity. Government authorities, landlords, banks, jobs, and EU border control — all accept them under that framework, so it is an oversight to treat such a document as untruthful.

What remains questionable is how this “financial institution” treats people rebuilding their lives.

I want to warn others about my extremely serious and disappointing experience with Revolut.

I was the victim of an armed robbery by individuals wearing balaclavas who forced me to make transfers from my Revolut account. The situation was treated as an emergency and I immediately called 999. The Metropolitan Police attended the scene and the crime was officially registered. I was given a CAD number and an official crime reference number.

I provided Revolut with all the information requested, including the CAD number and the police crime reference number. I even went personally to the police station and contacted the police again via the 101 service to ask if they could issue any additional documentation Revolut asked for. The Metropolitan Police clearly explained that in cases like this they cannot issue any further documents beyond the CAD number and the crime reference number.

Despite this being a violent robbery and despite the fact that I reported everything immediately and cooperated fully, Revolut has provided one of the worst customer support experiences I have ever encountered from a financial institution.

There is no proper phone support, no real assistance, and the communication has been extremely slow and unhelpful. I even attempted to report and block the transfers shortly after the incident, yet I still received no meaningful help.

What is most concerning is that Revolut appears willing to close or reject cases even when customers provide all available police evidence. Expecting documents that the police themselves confirm do not exist is completely unreasonable.

This experience has left me feeling completely unsupported as a victim of a serious crime.

I strongly advise people to be very careful when relying on a bank that does not provide proper human support in emergency situations. When something serious happens, you may find yourself completely alone trying to resolve it.

I sincerely hope Revolut improves the way they handle cases involving victims of crime, because the current level of support is unacceptable for a financial institution.

My story. I run a small business in the EEA. Due to recent geopolitical events and citizenship-related sanctions, I made the decision to close the company. The final step was withdrawing the remaining funds from my Revolut Business account.

1. Contacting Support

The particular complication in my case was that I no longer had any active European bank accounts, except for my personal Revolut account.

Revolut stated they were willing to transfer the funds to a non-European account, but required the receiving bank to support IBAN “for security reasons.” SWIFT transfers to an account number without IBAN were considered “unsafe” by them — something I learned only through this process.

My resident country’s bank does not support IBAN.

I spent one and a half months trying to resolve this with support. Both options — transferring to my personal Revolut account or sending a SWIFT transfer without IBAN — were rejected. The response was consistently “terribly sorry,” but no workable alternative was offered.

Support operates strictly by scripts, which is only part of the problem. The bigger issue is competence. My case was repeatedly “escalated to a special team that handles such matters,” only for another member of that “special team” to escalate it again to yet another “special team.” This cycle continued without progress.

I assumed they could not simply retain my funds indefinitely and would eventually find a solution.

I was wrong.

2. Contacting Revolut Compliance

I filed a formal complaint with Revolut’s Compliance department and attached the full support conversation.

The response restated the timeline of my communication and concluded — once again — with “terribly sorry,” and a suggestion that I was free to pursue legal action if I wished.

At this point, I expected Compliance — unlike frontline support — to take a reputational or regulatory perspective on the issue.

Again, I was mistaken.

3. Complaint to the Lithuanian Financial Ombudsman

I submitted a formal complaint to the Lithuanian financial ombudsman (which can be done remotely online). I included my correspondence with support, my compliance complaint, and their response.

Only after this external escalation did Revolut begin actively working on a solution.

Interestingly, it turned out that there are employees who are capable of resolving non-standard issues — and not just saying “terribly sorry.”

An alternative solution was eventually offered. It was not ideal, but I accepted it in order to finally conclude my relationship with Revolut.

I do not plan to resume it ever.

Summary

The withdrawal process took two and a half months.

Revolut’s service is convenient — as long as everything works within standard scenarios.

However, when facing a non-standard issue, support appears unable to act beyond scripted responses. Some representatives lack a basic understanding of how their own product functions.

It is possible to resolve complex issues — but be prepared for prolonged delays and potential escalation outside the organization. External escalation does work, but it costs time.

I hope the company improves its internal escalation procedures to avoid similar situations in the future.

At the end of December, I was scammed for €1,200. At the beginning of January, I was scammed again for €2,300 by a different individual. In both situations, I reported the transactions the very next day.
Although the app displayed scam warnings before the payments, similar warnings appear on many transactions. This makes it difficult to distinguish between a routine notice and a serious, high-risk situation. A general warning does not replace effective fraud prevention or a strong recovery process.
What concerns me most is the lack of transparency and communication. I was not properly informed about what actions were being taken, whether attempts were made to recover the funds, or what the outcome of the investigations actually was. I felt left without clear updates or support.
In contrast, when I experienced a similar issue with my local bank, they immediately opened a dispute, actively pursued the case, kept me updated, and successfully recovered my money. This demonstrates that proactive fraud handling and customer communication are possible.
Given that Revolut operates under EU financial regulations, I expected a much higher standard of consumer protection, transparency, and case management. Unfortunately, my experience did not reflect that.
I would appreciate a clear public response from Revolut explaining what specific actions were taken in my cases and whether any further review or recovery options remain available.
Despite their written promise, they did not help, they did not understand or investigate the real cause of the problem, and they just keep repeating excuses.

Revolut has gone from what I once considered the number one fintech to one of the most unreliable financial platforms I have ever used.

Both my personal and business accounts were suddenly frozen for 10 days under the justification of a “security check”. No prior warning. No meaningful explanation. No realistic timeline. Just a complete block on access to my own funds.

For a business account, this is not a minor inconvenience — it is operationally destructive. Payments could not be executed. Financial commitments were delayed. Transactions were disrupted. Cash flow was effectively paralysed. The damage caused by freezing funds for 10 days is not theoretical — it is real and immediate.

What is most concerning is the absolute lack of accountability and communication during the process. When a financial institution positions itself as a serious alternative to traditional banks, it cannot treat clients’ deposits as if they are optional access funds.

Security and compliance checks are understandable. Freezing both personal and business accounts simultaneously, without transparency and without urgency, is not.

This experience has fundamentally changed my view of Revolut. I will never again leave a significant balance on this platform. I strongly urge anyone using Revolut for business or holding substantial deposits to reconsider their exposure. If access to your funds can be removed without warning for extended periods, the platform simply cannot be relied upon as a primary banking solution.

Trust is the foundation of financial services. Once that trust is broken, it is very difficult to restore.

My recent experience with Revolut in handling an unauthorized transaction has been extremely disappointing and concerning.

On 15 January 2026, I was charged USD 328.89 by a hotel in Barbados while I was physically in Belgium. This was clearly an unauthorized transaction. However, because Revolut processed it as a delayed payment, the app did not offer me the option to report it as fraud. The only option available was to open a merchant dispute.

I was reassured by Revolut customer support (both AI and human agents) that this did not matter, that they would investigate properly, and that the case would be reclassified as fraud if needed. On 29 January, Revolut’s AI assistant even explicitly confirmed in writing that my case had been reclassified as fraudulent.

I was also instructed by Revolut to contact the hotel directly to try to resolve the issue. I followed their instructions in good faith.

However, my case was ultimately rejected. Even more concerning, Revolut later justified their refusal in part by arguing that because I had been in contact with the merchant, I could potentially be “working with the merchant.” This is shocking, considering that contacting the merchant was something Revolut themselves told me to do.

In addition, Revolut’s Complaints Analyst later contradicted their own AI assistant and stated that the case had never been treated as fraud, and used this as justification to refuse reimbursement. This means that I followed Revolut’s instructions at every step, relied on their guidance, and am now being penalized for it.

This also appears inconsistent with Mastercard rules. Under Mastercard Reason Code 4837 (“No Cardholder Authorization”), when a cardholder confirms they did not authorize or participate in a transaction, the issuer should treat it as fraud. I provided proof that I was in Belgium and could not have authorized a card-present transaction in Barbados.

Instead of protecting their customer, Revolut relied entirely on the merchant’s claim, while disregarding their own prior statements and guidance.

This experience has seriously undermined my trust in Revolut. Customers should be aware that:

– The app may prevent you from reporting fraud in certain situations;
– Customer support may reassure you that this does not matter, but those assurances may later be ignored;
– You may be instructed to contact the merchant, and then have that used against you;
– Even when fraud is reported immediately and supported by evidence, reimbursement may still be refused.

I strongly encourage Revolut to review how they handle fraud cases and how their AI and customer support guide customers in these critical situations.

Any other legitimate organisation would have threatened to terminate the call but I missed that red flag too.

So I reported that they (scammers) had a view of my account (in the Revolut app) which is why I believed them. I could see the balance being increased with each transaction and that overrode all the reservations I had.

This is a formal public complaint regarding Revolut’s decision to maintain a CIFAS marker against my name despite clear evidence that I am a victim of fraud.

Nine months ago, I was manipulated by an individual I met on Facebook who introduced me to cryptocurrency trading. I was completely new to crypto and did not understand the risks. This individual gained my trust and persuaded me to allow funds to pass through my Revolut account, claiming they were from his uncle and needed to be transferred urgently due to “account issues.”

Unexpectedly, I received five or six separate payments into my account. I was confused and questioned it. I was pressured and repeatedly reassured that everything was legitimate. Without criminal intent, and under manipulation, I transferred the funds as instructed.

Shortly afterwards, Revolut reported the matter to the police.

I was investigated for six months. I fully cooperated with law enforcement, provided all communications, and disclosed every piece of evidence in my possession. After completing their investigation, the police confirmed that I was a victim of fraud.

Despite this official conclusion, Revolut has maintained a CIFAS marker against my name for nine months.

This has had severe consequences:
• Significant damage to my financial standing
• Restrictions affecting my ability to access banking services
• Reputational harm
• Serious impact on my mental health, including hospital visits due to stress

I have supplied Revolut with the police report and all supporting documentation. There is no evidence of criminal intent on my part. Maintaining a CIFAS marker in circumstances where law enforcement has determined victim status raises serious concerns about fairness, proportionality, and regulatory compliance.

Financial institutions have a duty to prevent fraud — but they also have a duty to act reasonably, fairly, and in accordance with evidence. Continuing to penalise a confirmed victim is unjust and disproportionate.

If this matter is not urgently reviewed and resolved, I will have no option but to escalate formally through the appropriate regulatory and legal channels.

I am requesting:
1. Immediate review of my case by a senior compliance officer
2. Written justification for the continued CIFAS marker despite police findings
3. Prompt removal of the marker if no evidence of wrongdoing exists

I trusted Revolut as my financial institution. I did not expect to be treated as a criminal after being cleared by law enforcement.

I am asking for fairness, accountability, and the restoration of my financial reputation.
Good and fast

My experience with Revolut has been extremely disappointing and frustrating.

I was a victim of fraud, and €1639 was withdrawn from my account. I reported it immediately and was told that an investigation and fund recovery process had been initiated since 28/01/2026. However, to this day, I have not received any concrete evidence that a genuine attempt was made to recover my money.

The problem was not only the loss of the amount but also the way the case was handled:
• I was told my case was a “top priority,” yet there were no tangible results.
• I was informed that a recovery process was ongoing, without any proof to support it.
• I was refused a clear, traceable case reference number.
• Responses from different agents were contradictory.
• Timelines kept changing without clear explanation.

Overall, there is a clear lack of transparency and accountability.
Generic statements such as “we care about our customers” do not substitute for actual evidence or clear action.

Losing €1639 is not trivial, and the way this case was handled added to the frustration and psychological pressure instead of providing real support.

Based on my experience, I would not recommend dealing with them if you expect clarity, real protection, and transparent response in the event of a financial problem

After some calculations, I discovered that Revolut had factored in a 10% profit margin. An additional 1.5% fee was also charged. To see the spread between the buying and selling price, you have to have gold in your account. Then you can click the "Sell" button to see the actual selling price. Clicking the "Buy" button also displays the exact price. You can then calculate the spread yourself. This is a deliberate user manipulation tactic that Revolut uses to make money. Because of this trickery, I lost €150 on a small gold trade. I contacted customer service, but it was to no avail. I suspect the bank's app is intentionally designed this way.

We have been using Revolut Business as one of our primary business accounts for three years now. For three years, we had zero issues: standard inquiry procedures, reasonable deadlines, and no blocks.

On February 2nd, 2026, without any prior notice, I noticed a card payment failed. The card was blocked. "Okay," I thought, "I just need to unblock it." After digging a bit deeper, it turned out the entire account was fully restricted. No email notifications, no in-app alerts, no document requests, and no timeframe provided for the review. Nothing.

The support response was: "This is a standard security review procedure; please wait 1 to 5 business days."

I figured these things happen. A block is unpleasant, but if that’s the case, we’ll wait. We informed our clients, whose funds were effectively frozen, that they would need to wait 1 to 5 business days.

Today is the 5th business day. Aside from generic support replies claiming this is "normal," we have received ABSOLUTELY NOTHING—no official requirements, no explanations. We cannot withdraw funds; the account is simply dead.

Is this normal? You be the judge. We have extensive experience dealing with bank AML (Anti-Money Laundering) procedures, and this total lack of communication is staggering, as is the absence of any reasonable explanations or requests.

And this company is planning an IPO?

We have already filed a formal complaint with Revolut. Next week, we will be submitting a complaint to the regulator.
My advice to everyone? Do not open accounts with Revolut if you were planning to, because one fine day they might block everything without explanation. And if you are already using them, think carefully about whether you really need this kind of risk.

One star — avoid. Prolonged complaint failure, safeguarding breaches, and serious risk to vulnerable customers

My experience with Revolut has been genuinely alarming. A serious complaint has been ongoing since 28 November 2025 and remains unresolved months later, despite a formal written complaint, repeated chasers, and escalation to the Financial Ombudsman Service.

I experienced unreliable access to my own money, declined transactions despite sufficient funds, contradictory system notifications, and complete uncertainty over whether basic payments would work. That alone is unacceptable for a financial institution.

What makes this far worse is Revolut’s complaint handling. I was passed endlessly between agents, forced to repeat distressing information, given no continuity, no accountable complaints manager, and no meaningful responses. At times, my complaint appeared to be closed or “reopened” without my consent or explanation.

I disclosed terminal cancer, social anxiety disorder, and generalised anxiety disorder early on. Despite this, no safeguarding measures or reasonable adjustments were applied. An interaction with a staff member became so inappropriate that I felt intimidated and unsafe engaging with my own bank. After that incident, Revolut effectively went silent.

Having to deal with this prolonged failure, hostility, and lack of accountability while seriously ill has been emotionally and psychologically devastating. The stress caused by Revolut’s handling has significantly worsened my mental health, and I am now experiencing symptoms consistent with post-traumatic stress as a result of this ordeal.

Even basic legal obligations were mishandled. Data access requests were repeatedly delivered in inaccessible formats and never properly remedied.

After months of delay, silence, and procedural failure, I have been forced to escalate formally, including legal action, simply to obtain accountability.

This has been one of the most distressing consumer experiences of my life. Revolut’s complaints handling, safeguarding, and governance are not fit for purpose. Based on my experience, I would strongly urge others — especially vulnerable customers — to think very carefully before trusting this company with their money.

The verification process is a mess: by email, support said EEA ID cards and Swiss residence permits can be accepted, but in the app the only upload option shown is “Passport”. After multiple attempts, chat support confirmed their “final position” is that for Portuguese citizens opening an account in Switzerland, a Portuguese passport is the only document they can accept — no manual verification and no option to use a Portuguese Citizen Card (national ID) or Swiss residence permit.

This is extremely frustrating and inconsistent. In Switzerland, residents commonly use their residence permit and national ID for identification, so blocking onboarding unless you have a passport is unreasonable and should be clearly stated upfront.

How Revolut can improve:

Show the real document requirements before starting onboarding (especially if “passport only” applies).

Align email/chat/app information so customers don’t waste time.

Enable Portuguese ID card and/or Swiss residence permit for verification in Switzerland, or provide a genuine manual verification route.

My experience with Revolut continues to deteriorate, and the core problem is no longer a single incident but systemic unreliability.

Over time, I have faced repeated cases of incompetent and inconsistent customer support, where agents provide contradictory or plainly incorrect information. Issues are escalated, but rarely resolved, and follow-ups often lead nowhere.

What is most concerning is the lack of predictability and transparency. Restrictions and limitations appear without clear explanations, timelines, or guidance. As a customer, it becomes impossible to understand what is allowed, what is restricted, and why. This creates unnecessary stress and exposes users to avoidable financial risk — not because of market conditions, but due to internal operational failures.

Revolut’s complaint handling process is ineffective. Concerns are acknowledged formally, yet meaningful resolution is absent. Escalations feel procedural rather than substantive, leaving customers without clarity or accountability.

Instead of feeling supported by a regulated financial institution, I feel I am dealing with a fragmented system where no one takes ownership of the outcome. For a company positioning itself as a modern bank and investment platform, this level of service is unacceptable.

Based on my prolonged experience, I cannot consider Revolut a reliable place to manage funds or plan financial actions. I would strongly advise others to be cautious and consider alternatives with clearer rules, better support, and genuine customer accountability.

One of the worst banking experiences I’ve ever had.

After being the victim of a brutal robbery, I did exactly what you are supposed to do: I reported the crime to the police, documented everything, and fully cooperated with the bank. I provided all information and evidence they requested, including the police report.

Instead of receiving support, protection, or even basic understanding, I was met with suspicion and blame. Despite clear evidence that I was a victim of crime, the bank chose to close my account, accusing me of “misusing dispute services.” This accusation is completely unfounded and deeply distressing, especially given the circumstances.

At a time when I was already traumatized, the bank’s response added further stress and insecurity. There was no meaningful investigation, no real dialogue, and no attempt to resolve the matter fairly. I was treated as a liability rather than a customer in need of help.

A financial institution should protect its customers — particularly victims of violent crime — not punish them for reporting fraud and following proper procedures. This experience has left me feeling abandoned, disrespected, and unfairly blamed.

I cannot recommend this bank to anyone who expects basic human decency, due process, or support in a crisis.

I had a very disappointing experience with Revolut, related to incorrect and misleading communication about savings interest rates.

I received an official email stating that the interest rate on my existing PLN savings account would drop from 5% to 4% on 19 January, while new PLN savings accounts would have a 5% rate. This clearly suggested that opening a new account was a valid way to retain the higher rate.

Based on this email, and following explicit instructions from Revolut support, I closed my existing savings accounts, opened a new one, and moved my funds. It later turned out that the email was wrong and that the 5% rate never applied.

As a direct consequence of following Revolut’s guidance, my funds were temporarily left outside any savings account, causing lost interest. More importantly, by being forced to reopen a new savings account under different conditions, I am now subject to a lower cap than before and will earn less interest going forward than I would have if I had done nothing.

After raising the issue with their support team and providing a copy of the email I had received, Revolut refused to take responsibility for the consequences of their communication and the guidance provided. The final response was essentially: “The applicable interest rate is 4%; your previous balance above the current account cap cannot be restored, and there is nothing we can do.”

This experience seriously undermined my trust. When it comes to important financial matters, misleading information followed by a lack of accountability is unacceptable. The support experience felt procedural and dismissive rather than customer-focused.

The conclusion of this experience is that, when important financial decisions depend on their communication and support, Revolut fails to offer the level of reliability and responsibility that customers should reasonably expect.

Do not make the mistake of using Revolut Business or Revolut X for your cryptocurrency investments. While they make it incredibly easy to "buy" and deposit money, they make it a nightmare to actually own your assets.

Here is the "Scam" cycle they put you through:

The "Hostage" Restriction: The moment you try to move a significant amount of crypto to your own cold wallet (self-custody), they flag your account for a "security review." Your funds are effectively frozen while they ask for endless documentation.

The Limit Trap: Once they "unblock" you, they often apply ridiculously low daily withdrawal limits (sometimes as low as £2,000). If you have a large position, it will take you weeks or months of daily manual effort to move your own money out.

The Fee Bleed: Every single one of these small mandatory transactions carries a "Revolut Service Fee" (usually £5 per transaction for BTC/ETH ) plus the network fee. If you are forced to move £50,000 in tiny £2000 increments, you are paying Revolut thousands of pounds in fees just to withdraw your own money.

No Private Keys: You do not own the keys on Revolut. They act as a middleman that profits from your inability to leave.

Conclusion: If you value your financial freedom, stay away. Revolut Business is designed to keep your capital hostage while slowly draining it through forced, high-frequency fees.

Not your keys, not your coins. Avoid at all costs.

I’ve been a Revolut Premium/Metal customer for nearly 10 years.

Revolut works well for day-to-day banking. But if something non-standard goes wrong, support becomes a serious problem.

I was double-charged more than €1,500 at a hotel due to a technical error. The hotel system rejected the transaction, yet the amount was still debited from my account and never returned.

Since then, I’ve spent months dealing with Revolut support. I had to explain the same issue repeatedly because cases are constantly passed between agents. The problem was often misunderstood. When the case was escalated, there was no way to communicate with the team handling it and no way to correct misunderstandings once they occurred.

Despite multiple escalations and a formal complaint, Revolut refused to resolve what is effectively an orphaned transaction: the money left my account, but no valid settlement exists.

I have now initiated external proceedings with the Deutsche Bundesbank.

Bottom line: Revolut is convenient when everything works. But if a larger amount is involved and the issue falls outside a simple script, you should be prepared for a long, opaque, and frustrating process with very limited accountability.

Title: Violent assault, coercion and non-authorised payments – two refusals despite EU law

I want to clearly explain what happened to me, as this is not a simple fraud case.

On 17 September 2024, I was the victim of a violent assault. My phone was stolen and I was subjected to serious threats. Under extreme pressure, I was forced to disclose security codes against my will. I was not acting freely and did not consent to any financial operation.

Following this assault, my attackers used my stolen phone to initiate several card payments and a transfer from my Revolut account using a Revolut-issued card. I never authorised these transactions.

On 17 December 2025, I formally disputed these transactions with Revolut. This dispute was rejected.
On 26 December 2025, I submitted a formal complaint following the refusal of the dispute. This complaint was also rejected, with a response advising me to contact another “issuing bank”, which is legally incoherent since Revolut issued the card and processed the payments.

Under Article L133-18 of the French Monetary and Financial Code and the EU PSD2 Directive, payments made under coercion following theft and violence are classified as non-authorised payments and must be reimbursed unless gross negligence is proven. No such negligence has been demonstrated in my case.

Throughout this process, I experienced major difficulties reaching the appropriate fraud and complaints teams, received mostly standardised responses, and faced excessive delays despite the seriousness of the situation and its impact on my mental health.

I am still seeking an amicable internal resolution and sincerely hope this matter can be resolved fairly and in accordance with the law. However, time is pressing. If this situation remains unresolved, I will escalate the case to journalists, consumer protection organisations, influencers and media outlets to ensure that this failure to uphold European consumer protection rights is made public.

I have been a loyal Revolut customer for many years, but their recent decline in customer service and transparency has been extremely disappointing.
Recently, I made a couple of consecutive cash withdrawals overseas using my Revolut card. The ATM gave no warning about any additional fees either displayed on the screen or on the printed receipts. Later, I was shocked to see on the Revolut app that one transaction had a fee of around 13%.
I immediately contacted Revolut. The agent acknowledged my frustration and agreed that the information was unclear and unfair. They advised me to raise a dispute so that they can help and get in touch with the overseas bank, which is a famous bank, which I did, providing evidence (including a receipt showing the withdrawal amount without any fee).
A day later, Revolut informed me they had contacted the merchant but received no response, so they would process the dispute themselves. Hours later, I received a short, vague message stating the fee was “within the rules” and they could not help. No proper explanation, no accountability.

This clearly demonstrates a lack of transparency and that Revolut failed to protect its customers from hidden charges, Revolut is my card issuer and they should not deal with such banks that mislead their customers.

This experience shows that Revolut is not prioritizing customer protection or clear communication. We are a small business Revolut Business customer and are extremely disappointed with how Revolut has handled a rejected international payment and its subsequent return.

On 11 November, a £2,160 international transfer was rejected and never reached the beneficiary. Revolut have repeatedly confirmed this themselves. However, despite the standard return timeframe (up to 20 business days) passing long ago, the funds have still not been returned to our account.

Over the following weeks, we have:

engaged repeatedly with Revolut support,

had SWIFT traces and recalls opened,

been told MT199 messages were sent to partner banks,

submitted a “Locate Incoming Transfer” escalation,

been escalated to Payments Ops and then “core payments”,

and been given multiple rolling “1 day” ETAs that were never met.

Despite all of this, Revolut have been unable or unwilling to provide:

a clear timeline for resolution,

a defined escalation point if partner banks do not respond,

or any meaningful updates beyond generic “in review” statuses in the app.

At this stage, the only consistent advice has been to “wait” and “check the app”, while our business remains without access to funds that should have been returned weeks ago. We were ultimately forced to submit a formal complaint simply to obtain accountability and written responses.

The most concerning aspect is not that an international transfer encountered an issue — these things can happen — but that Revolut appear to have no effective process for resolving missing returns within a reasonable timeframe, nor any ability to communicate clearly with affected customers when cash flow is at risk.

This situation has caused genuine financial stress and disruption to our small business, including concerns around meeting payroll. We are continuing to pursue resolution through formal complaint channels, but based on our experience, we cannot currently rely on Revolut Business for time‑critical international payments or fund recovery.

Edit: in response to Revolut’s response: that message is almost identical to the past 6 weeks worth of messages, and gets us nowhere.

If you are a developer or business attempting to integrate Revolut's Merchant API, prepare for a nightmare.

We spent days debugging a persistent 401 Unauthenticated error on their Production environment. We followed their documentation to the letter:

Used the correct Production Secret Keys (which we regenerated 3 separate times at support's request).
Confirmed standard Authorization: Bearer headers were perfectly formatted.
Verified our integration against their Sandbox (which worked fine).
The issue was definitively proven to be a backend permission error on Revolut's side. We provided them with verbose curl logs from a local machine showing that even a brand-new, fresh key was being rejected with a 401 error instantly. This is undeniable proof that the account itself is blocked or misconfigured on their end.

Instead of escalating this to an engineer who could flip the switch, their first-line support spent hours gaslighting us. They forced us to regenerate keys repeatedly, asked for code snippets we had already validated, and blamed "IP Whitelisting" despite our dashboard having no option to configure IPs.

They completely refused to accept technical evidence or check our account's API scope status. If you need a payment partner that actually works and has support staff who know what an API header is, use Stripe or Adyen. Revolut’s API is a black box, and their support team has no keys to open it.

I’m writing this review out of frustration, but also with the hope that someone at Revolut finally pays attention.

I applied to the Revolut affiliate program via Impact in September. Since then, I have not received any acceptance, rejection, or meaningful response. What is more disappointing is that I have sent multiple emails to the contact address provided for affiliates, and none of them have been answered. We are now in December.

For context, I am not a beginner affiliate. I run a travel guide ranked number one on Google for a major Asian destination, with high-intent visitors who are actively looking for travel cards, banking solutions, and fintech products like Revolut. I currently work with more than twelve affiliate programs, and Revolut is the only one where I’ve experienced this level of silence and lack of professionalism.

On top of that, I am a Revolut customer myself since February and I am actively testing and using the card. This makes the situation even more frustrating, as I’m genuinely interested in promoting the product, not just applying blindly.

I understand that affiliate applications can be selective or delayed, but not replying at all for months is simply poor customer and partner support. A simple “yes”, “no”, or “we’re reviewing your application” would have been enough.

This review is not written just to complain, but to ask Revolut to seriously improve communication and responsiveness, especially with potential partners who are already customers and are bringing real, qualified traffic.

I hope someone from Revolut’s support or affiliate team finally takes action.

***Update after Revolut’s public reply:

What I find particularly concerning is that Revolut is extremely quick to respond publicly to negative reviews on Trustpilot, yet does not respond at all to direct emails from people genuinely interested in promoting their business, even after several months.

Answering your previous reply and after having checked the info and FAQs provided on: revolut[com]/en-US/become-a-revolut-affiliate/ nowhere is indicated that "due to the high volume of...." or similar. It clearly states in the bottom of the page:

Get in touch
For affiliate queries, please reach out to affiliates[at]revolut.com.

So the expected thing is a proper reply.

To be very clear:
I am not only an affiliate applicant, I am also a Revolut customer and an active user of the card. I have been trying to contact Revolut through the official affiliate contact email clearly provided for enquiries, for over three months, without receiving a single reply.

If Revolut replies to me through that same email channel I have been using since September and provides a clear answer, I will gladly remove this review. My intention is not to damage the brand, but to receive a basic level of communication.

I sincerely hope this can be resolved properly. Otherwise, I will have to reconsider continuing as a Revolut customer, as responsiveness and transparency are fundamental in a financial service.
