After several years as a Revolut customer, I've closed both my personal and business accounts. The final straw came when they suddenly terminated my girlfriend's account without warning or adequate explanation, cutting off her access to our joint account in the process. The way they handled this situation—and their complete lack of accountability—convinced me this company doesn't deserve my business.

My experience with Revolut:

Arbitrary account closures: My girlfriend's account was shut down with minimal explanation. Despite her attempts to resolve the issue, she received generic responses and no clear path to reinstatement. This wasn't just an inconvenience—it meant having to set up new direct debits for running the house as when we tried to move to Monzo, they blocked the switch (twice).

Money transfers treated like criminal activity: I attempted to send £50,000 to a family member to repay a personal loan I no longer needed. Revolut held the funds for over a day while demanding excessive documentation and verification. This wasn't a suspicious transaction—it was a straightforward transfer to a family member (who had originally sent me the money). The cynic in me suspects they simply wanted to earn interest on the money while I jumped through their hoops.

Pointless identity verification theatre: Every few weeks, I receive urgent emails and app notifications threatening account closure if I don't "complete my identity details immediately." The punchline? Roughly 9 out of 10 times, when you click through, the app tells you nothing is actually required. No traditional bank operates this way. It's either incompetence or a deliberate tactic to keep users anxiously engaged with the app—either way, it's exhausting.

"Premier" support that isn't: I paid for Premier support expecting, well, premier service. What I got was the same slow, unhelpful responses as standard support, just with a fancier label. When I actually needed help with serious issues, the response was cookie-cutter templates and delays.
The bottom line: After years of tolerating these issues, the account closure was the wake-up call I needed. I've moved to Monzo, where I'm treated like a customer rather than a potential fraudster.

Save yourself the frustration. There are better options out there.

I never imagined a financial platform could put a legitimate business in such a vulnerable position.

Our company, Mega Minds Tech Ltd, has a Revolut Business account currently under review, and for over a week, we have been completely locked out of our own funds — £17,500 frozen with no clear explanation or timeline.

The consequences are devastating:

• Employee salaries are delayed
• Our Meta Ads account was restricted due to failed payments
• Incoming payouts are on hold
• Daily business operations are paralyzed

What hurts the most is not the review itself — compliance checks are understandable.
What hurts is the silence and uncertainty.

We have contacted support multiple times. Responses are polite, but repetitive:
“Please wait. The process is ongoing.”

No clear list of required documents.
No estimated resolution time.
No sense of urgency — while our business bleeds daily.

We are a real UK-registered business, with invoices, contracts, payout proofs — everything ready. If something is needed, tell us clearly. We are more than willing to cooperate.

When a business account is frozen, it’s not just money that’s locked —
it’s employees, families, and livelihoods.

I truly hope Revolut understands the real-world impact of such delays and improves communication and accountability for business customers.

At this point, we are not asking for favors —
we are asking for clarity, transparency, and access to our own funds.

I’m shocked at how Revolut handled my recent fraud case. Multiple Depop transactions were made that I did not authorise, yet Revolut closed the investigation in less than 72 hours. Under Mastercard rules and FCA/PSD2 regulations, the correct process is to raise a chargeback, liaise with the merchant, obtain evidence such as delivery address, device logs, IP history, and proof of fulfilment a process that can take up to 12 weeks. Instead, Revolut relied solely on their own internal assessment and refused to perform a full investigation.

Their own advisor even admitted that accounts can be compromised or “pinched,” which directly contradicts the decision to treat the transactions as authorised. I was also given incorrect information, including being told Mastercard made a “final decision” which is simply not true. For unauthorised transactions, the liability sits with the bank unless they can prove the customer authorised the payment. Revolut provided no such proof.

Situations like this are exactly why people say the system keeps the poor struggling while large financial companies protect their own interests. Customers lose money while the bank refuses to follow the correct dispute process.

It’s also hard to ignore the wider picture. Revolut’s founder and CEO, Nik Storonsky, recently moved his tax residency to the UAE a decision widely reported to potentially save him billions in UK capital gains tax when Revolut eventually goes public. Reports also stated that this relocation was not immediately communicated to UK regulators, prompting further scrutiny. Meanwhile, ordinary customers like me are left unprotected, blamed, and dismissed when fraud occurs. It really does feel like a perfect example of how the wealthy at the top protect their own interests while ordinary people are left to absorb losses with no support. When a founder can relocate to save billions in tax, yet the company cannot refund a few hundred pounds of obvious fraud, the priorities become very clear.

I have been using Revolut for several years, but a recent experience was so shockingly poor that I feel compelled to write this review. Shortly after my phone was stolen, I noticed a transaction on my account that immediately alarmed me. Two small amounts had been moved from a foreign currency exchange into GBP, and then the entire balance was transferred to a third-party account I did not recognise — an account with Lloyds Banking Group. When I reported that I did not make this transaction, the communication from Revolut’s chat agent was extremely unclear. I was told I needed to call Lloyds Banking Group because it was a “call back,” but this was never explained in a way that made sense. I am not familiar with internal banking terminology, and instead of receiving proper guidance, I felt dismissed and brushed off.Naturally, I assumed the transaction was fraudulent and expressed my concerns. Instead of helping me understand what had happened, Revolut placed the account under review and then closed it. I still have no idea what truly occurred — only that they closed my account without any hesitation. It felt almost personal rather than a professional, considered decision.It is also important to note that when a bank closes an account in this manner, it can leave a marker on the customer’s record, which may impact future banking. That alone is concerning. To add to this, I have a friend who had £90,000 stolen from his Revolut account after his phone was also stolen, and he is now in court with the company. My own experience, combined with what I have seen happen to others, makes it clear: if Revolut does not like something you say or if there is any misunderstanding, they simply close the account. There is no proper escalation process, no person to call, and everything is handled through a chat system. If you don’t understand something, the conversation effectively ends.Having your phone stolen is already stressful and can make anyone feel vulnerable, yet Revolut showed no empathy and no willingness to communicate clearly. It felt as though their attitude mattered more than helping a customer in distress.
I can honestly say I am relieved that I will no longer be banking with them — especially knowing that if a larger sum of money had been involved, the outcome could have been far worse.

I am deeply disappointed with Revolut’s handling of its recent refer a business, earn £400 promotion. (Support case number: 92066-75372-16763)
My business regularly advises other companies, and I often recommend Revolut Business accounts without expecting any reward. For the first time, I decided to participate in this promotion. The referred business completed all the required steps within the stated deadline—or so we thought. The final step was completed on the day of the deadline, but Revolut later claimed the cut-off was 9pm, not midnight.
To make matters worse, technical issues on Revolut’s side prevented the new account from making a required payment earlier that day. Despite this, Revolut refused to honour the promised £400, stating the last step was completed two hours outside the deadline. This rigid stance is unacceptable, especially when the delay was caused by Revolut’s own technical problems.
What concerns me most is the shift in Revolut’s attitude. The promotion was clearly designed to expand Revolut’s business customer base—and I delivered exactly that. Yet Revolut chose to deny the reward, hiding behind a technicality. Ironically, the very next day, I received another email advertising the same £400 referral campaign, proving that their push for new business customers continues.
This behaviour demonstrates a worrying loss of market acumen and agility, qualities that once defined Revolut’s success. If Revolut cannot honour its own promotions, I see no reason to continue recommending them. I will now advise my clients to open accounts with Monzo or other neobanks, and encourage existing Revolut clients to transfer their business elsewhere.
This is not about £400. I was happy to refer clients without rewards. It is about Revolut’s unsupportable refusal to honour its own offer, and the wasted time I spent helping my client overcome Revolut’s technical issues, and over an hour on low-quality support chat. Adding to the frustration, some of the links to the promotion’s terms and conditions were not even working.
My time is worth more than this. Revolut has lost my trust — and my referrals.

⭐ 1 out of 5 – Revolut held my own money hostage for hours and forced me to send private bank statements and answer humiliating questions just to withdraw it (19 November 2025)
Today I tried to make a completely normal withdrawal from my Revolut account to my own bank and the transaction was instantly paused “for security reasons”.
What followed was almost 3 hours of the worst customer “service” I have ever experienced from any bank:
• Multiple agents (names kept changing: Chat Assistant → Stacy → Jit → Abdul) demanded I upload screenshots, PDFs and full bank statements from my other banks via the in-app paperclip.
• They asked deeply personal and irrelevant questions such as “How long have you been investing in betting/gambling sites?”
• When I quoted Revolut’s own official policy (“We will NEVER ask you to send bank statements or screenshots via in-app chat” – still on their website today), they completely ignored it and kept repeating the same demands.

This is NOT source-of-funds verification done properly. This is bullying and humiliation until the customer gives in. Revolut’s own rules and UK/EU consumer laws say they cannot hold your money indefinitely while treating you like a criminal for perfectly legal activity.
I got my money in the end, but only because I spent hours fighting and sending private documents I should never have been asked for.
Revolut may be convenient for small day-to-day stuff, but the moment you try to move your own money out they treat you like this. I have now transferred everything to a proper high-street bank and will be closing my Revolut account.
If you keep any serious money in Revolut, be warned – getting it out can turn into a nightmare.

As a business owner with multiple limited companies, I’ve dealt with a wide range of business banking providers, and unfortunately, Revolut has been one of the worst experiences by comparison. Despite being among the more expensive options (and having subscribed to several of their premium plans), the level of service is almost non-existent. Everything is pushed through automation, making it extremely difficult (if not practically impossible) to speak to a real person when you genuinely need support.

Security is another major concern. I’ve had attempted fraudulent transactions on two separate Revolut business accounts, belonging to two different companies, both within the first few days of opening the accounts and before the physical cards had even arrived. This has severely undermined my confidence in Revolut’s security measures, and of course, there was no human support available when this happened either.

Their referral scheme also appears to be misleading at best. The incentive amounts keep increasing, yet the bonuses never seem to be paid. I have personally been denied over £1,800 in referral rewards, all marked as “failed” despite every requirement being met. Judging by other reviews, I am far from alone, and Revolut’s standard response is always that customers haven’t completed “all steps”, which feels like a convenient way for them to avoid paying out.

The app also prompts you for reviews constantly, which inevitably skews the overall ratings. Highly incentivised and repetitive review prompts should make anyone cautious about taking the published scores at face value.

At present, I hold four Revolut business accounts and one personal account. If anyone from Revolut actually reads this, I would genuinely appreciate a real human reaching out so I can close everything down, because there seems to be no other reliable way of getting help.

In conclusion: don’t waste your time with Revolut. Other challenger banks, and even traditional high street lenders, offer far better service, often at a lower cost. A formal Ombudsman complaint is currently in progress.

I was very happy with Revolut until last month.

It all started when I tried to send money to my Coinbase account. Revolut blocked the transaction as it was marked suspicious and asked me questions about it. Don’t get me wrong at that point I wasn’t frustrated, as I understand they have the responsibility to protect their customers from scammers. We chatted for a while and then they called me. I provided all the information they needed, except some of my private email threads, which I believe is an invasion of privacy. This whole process took around 4–5 hours. After that, the transaction was approved.

A few days later, I tried to send more money and they again started questioning me, even though I clearly explained what I was doing and it was obvious from the evidence I provided that I was not being scammed. They then rejected my transaction and told me there was no way I could send money to my Coinbase account from Revolut. I was quite angry at that point because I felt nobody was actually looking at the evidence I provided, and instead they were trusting their automated fraud detection system, which was naturally triggered by a crypto transaction.

After that, I tried to send the money to my Monzo account and they blocked this transaction as well, which made me furious. How can a bank prevent me from moving my own money to another account in my name? It took them more than 20 hours to resolve this situation.

I think fraud detection systems are useful in general, but in Revolut’s case the false positives are so frequent that it makes it impossible for me to use it as my main account anymore.

My Review of Revolut
I’m really disappointed with Revolut’s service. My account was closed — which is fine — but I had over £270 in it. They claimed the money was sent to another bank, but that bank never received it, and there’s no proof that the transfer was completed.

For the past two months, I’ve contacted Revolut repeatedly, but they haven’t helped me or provided any update. They keep saying they “can’t locate the funds,” even though they were the ones who had them in the first place.

When you’re still a customer, Revolut replies quickly and seems helpful. But once your account is closed, it feels like they stop caring — even about unresolved issues. I’ve already waited more than a month for a proper response, and all I get are the same automated replies telling me to “wait longer.”

I just want my money back. I have bills to pay and shouldn’t have to chase them endlessly. Be careful with this company — they don’t treat ex-customers fairly.
